intro: 1998. Marvel just crawled out of bankruptcy on its hands and knees. You own the most stacked character roster in entertainment — Spider-Man, X-Men, Iron Man — and basically zero cash. Hollywood thinks comic-book movies are a punchline. You run Marvel. Time to speedrun a redemption arc.

realOutcome.valuation: sold to Disney for $4B; the MCU has grossed $30B+
realOutcome.summary: Marvel went from bankrupt to inventing the biggest film franchise ever. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: You're broke and need cash NOW. Studios will pay upfront to license your characters — Fox wants X-Men, Sony wants Spider-Man — but the terms are lopsided in their favor.

choices.text: License heroes to studios to survive
choices.reveal: Marvel licensed its heroes — X-Men to Fox, Spidey to Sony — for upfront cash and tiny royalties. The deals were lopsided but they kept the lights on and proved the characters could carry blockbusters. Survival mode W.

choices.text: Hold all the rights and wait for a better day
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect the IP and run out of money defending it. A flawless character library owned by a dead company is worth exactly nothing. Diamond hands into the void.

choices.text: Sell the characters outright for a lump sum
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You sell the crown jewels for survival cash. Someone else builds the cinematic universe on YOUR heroes. Generational fumble.

decisions.scenario: X-Men and Spider-Man become massive hits — for the STUDIOS. You collect a tiny royalty while others make hundreds of millions on your characters. The math is genuinely enraging.

choices.text: Realize licensing leaves all the upside on the table
choices.reveal: Marvel watched studios print fortunes on its characters while it took a sliver. That painful realization set up the boldest move in company history. The 'we could just do this ourselves' moment.

choices.text: Keep licensing everything cheap for safe cash
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You stay a low-risk licensing shop forever, collecting crumbs while others feast. Safe, small, and permanently benched at your own game.

choices.text: Sue the studios to renegotiate the old deals
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You spend years and millions in court instead of building. Even if you win, every future partner now sees you as litigation waiting to happen.

decisions.scenario: You want to make your OWN films and keep the profits. To do it, you'd take a $525M loan from Merrill Lynch, using your remaining characters — including Iron Man — as collateral. Flop a movie and you could lose them.

choices.text: Take the risky loan and finance your own movies
choices.reveal: Marvel took the legendary $525M Merrill loan, pledging Iron Man and others as collateral, to self-finance its films. Betting the characters on ITSELF was the whole ballgame. All in, cards on the table.

choices.text: Stay a licensor, never risk the characters
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You play it safe and never control your own films. Marvel Studios and its $30B universe just... never exist. Safe and forgotten.

choices.text: Sell to a big studio now while the IP is hot
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cash out before proving you can make the movies yourself — and leave almost all the value on the table for the buyer. Sold the ticket before the number hit.

decisions.scenario: Your first self-made film needs a hero and a star. Safe pick: a famous A-lister and a household-name character. Your team wants to lead with Iron Man — a B-lister — played by Robert Downey Jr., who most studios think is too risky to even insure.

choices.text: Cast Robert Downey Jr. and lead with Iron Man
choices.reveal: Marvel bet its first film on a B-list hero and a 'high-risk' actor. Iron Man (2008) smashed, RDJ became the face of a franchise, and the post-credits scene launched a whole universe. Casting of all time.

choices.text: Lead with a safe, famous flagship hero instead
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A generic 'safe' launch gets a generic result. No RDJ spark, no audacity, no cultural moment. Mid the universe into existence and it flatlines.

choices.text: Cast a cheaper 'safer' actor to reduce risk
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You save on the star and lose the soul. The exact chemistry that made Iron Man work — and made the whole MCU possible — never happens.

decisions.scenario: Iron Man cooked. The universe is taking off, and Disney rolls up with a $4B offer for the whole company. Stay independent and keep it all — or take the bag.

choices.reveal: Marvel sold to Disney in 2009 for $4B. Disney's money and distribution turbocharged the MCU into the highest-grossing franchise in film history — insane outcome for a company that was bankrupt a decade earlier.

choices.text: Stay independent and keep all the upside
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You keep control but you don't have Disney's global machine. The MCU still grows, just slower and smaller, and you eat all the risk of every expensive bet solo.

choices.text: Sell to a rival studio for less, faster
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take a quicker, cheaper exit to a buyer without Disney's reach. The characters end up managed, not unleashed. Left money and legacy on the table.

bands.line: Bankruptcy to the biggest franchise in film. Legend episode: WON. Redemption arc complete.
A healthy licensing business — never the studio that changed cinema. Mid.
You gave the heroes away and stayed broke. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Marvel 16x'd by betting on itself. Today's agents flagged a company sitting on assets it's too scared to use →
